book_claims.claim: Deficiencies in B vitamins have been implicated in brain aging and dementias; Alzheimer's disease patients have lower levels of folate and vitamin B12.
book_claims.mechanism: B vitamins are essential for neuronal function, myelin sheath maintenance, and homocysteine metabolism; deficiency may accelerate brain degeneration by elevating homocysteine, a neurotoxic amino acid.

book_claims.claim: B vitamin supplementation was found largely ineffective for dementia prevention except in countries where food is not fortified with folate.
book_claims.mechanism: In countries with mandatory folate fortification, baseline B-vitamin status is often adequate, so supplementation provides marginal additional benefit.

supplementary_research.source: Smith et al., PLoS ONE (2010) — PMID 20838622
supplementary_research.finding: High-dose B-vitamin supplementation (folic acid, B6, B12) slowed brain atrophy rate by ~30% over 2 years in older adults with mild cognitive impairment and elevated homocysteine, suggesting a protective role against neurodegeneration.

B-vitamin treatment specifically reduced atrophy in brain regions most affected by Alzheimer's disease (medial temporal lobe), with greater benefit in those with higher baseline homocysteine, reinforcing the brain-aging connection.

Meta-analysis of 11 trials found that B-vitamin supplementation lowered homocysteine and significantly reduced stroke risk by ~7%, supporting cardiovascular longevity benefits beyond cognitive effects.

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: Varies by food source; dark leafy greens ~194 mcg
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Synthetic folic acid is ~85% bioavailable; food folate ~50%; absorption reduced by alcohol and some medications.

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: Clams ~98 mcg; fortified cereals ~6 mcg per serving
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Requires intrinsic factor for absorption; bioavailability declines with age due to reduced gastric acid; vegans at high risk of deficiency.
Bioavailability from plant foods ~75% vs ~83% from animal foods; can be destroyed by heat processing.

synergies.description: Both nutrients support neurological health; combined deficiency further increases dementia risk.
Omega-3 fatty acids and B vitamins together more effectively slow brain atrophy than either alone, as shown in the VITACOG trial.
Dark leafy greens are among the richest dietary sources of folate, making them a key food vehicle for B9 intake.
